title: Strings & Text
subtitle: Learn how Java holds words, names, and whole sentences — and the tricks to play with them.
goal: Create strings, join them together, measure and slice them, change their case, and compare them the RIGHT way using .equals().

blocks.heading: What is a String? ✍️
blocks.html:
A String is how Java stores text — a name, a word, a sentence, even a whole story. Any time you have letters, spaces, or symbols, you wrap them in double quotes "like this".

Think of a String like a row of beads on a thread. Each bead is one character: H, i, and so on. Java gives you lots of tools to peek at the beads and play with them. 🧵

blocks.heading: Making and joining strings
blocks.html:
To make a String, you write String name = "Mia";. The word on the left is the box's label; the text in quotes is what goes inside.

To glue strings together, use the + sign. We call this concatenation (a big word for "sticking text end to end"). You can also ask a String how long it is with .length().

blocks.caption: Create, join, and measure a string
blocks.output:
Hello, Mia!
Your name has 3 letters.
blocks.explain: The + glues "Hello, ", the name, and "!" into one string. name.length() counts the characters — Mia has 3.

blocks.html: Watch your spaces! Java glues text exactly as written. "Hello," + name gives Hello,Mia (squished). Add a space inside the quotes: "Hello, " to keep things tidy.

blocks.heading: Peeking inside: charAt, substring, indexOf
blocks.html:
Each character in a String has a position number called an index. Counting starts at 0, not 1! So in "Dragon", the D is at index 0, r is at index 1, and so on.

  • charAt(0) — grab one character at a spot.
  • substring(0, 4) — slice out a chunk (from index 0 up to but not including 4).
  • indexOf("g") — find where a letter lives.
  • toUpperCase() / toLowerCase() — SHOUT or whisper.

blocks.caption: Slicing and changing case
blocks.output:
First letter: D
Slice: Drag
Where is 'g'? 3
LOUD: DRAGON
quiet: dragon
blocks.explain: charAt(0) is the very first letter. substring(0, 4) grabs indexes 0,1,2,3 = "Drag". The 'g' sits at index 3. Case methods make a brand-new string in caps or lowercase.

blocks.html: Indexes start at 0! 🔢 The first character is at position 0, not 1. This trips up everyone at first — even grown-up programmers. Count from zero and you'll be a pro.

blocks.question: In the word "Dragon", what does charAt(0) give you?
blocks.explain: Indexes start at 0, so charAt(0) is the FIRST character — the letter D.

blocks.heading: Tidying up: trim and replace
blocks.html:
Sometimes text has messy extra spaces at the start or end. trim() snips those off. And replace("c", "b") swaps every c for a b.

Important: strings in Java are immutable — that means you can never change one in place. These methods don't edit the old string; they hand you a brand-new one. 🆕

blocks.heading: The big one: equals() vs ==
blocks.html:
To check if two strings have the same text, use .equals(), like a.equals(b). Do NOT use == to compare strings!

The == sign asks "are these the exact same box?" while .equals() asks "do these hold the same words?" — and you almost always want the words. This is the #1 String mistake, so remember: compare strings with .equals(). ⚠️

blocks.caption: equals, trim, replace, and String.format
blocks.output:
a equals b? true
[hi there]
bats
The fox has 4 legs.
blocks.explain: a.equals(b) is true because both hold "pizza". trim() removes the spaces (the [ ] show where the text starts and ends). replace swaps c for b: cats becomes bats. String.format fills in %s with a word and %d with a number.

blocks.html: %s and %d are blanks to fill in. In String.format and System.out.printf, %s means "put a string here" and %d means "put a whole number here." Java fills them in order, left to right.

blocks.question: What is the SAFE way to check if two strings hold the same text?
blocks.explain: Always use .equals() for strings. == checks if they are the same box in memory, which is usually NOT what you want.

blocks.question: Strings in Java are immutable. What does that mean?
options: Methods like trim() change the original string
Once made, a string can't be changed — methods return a NEW string
blocks.explain: Immutable means unchangeable. trim(), replace(), and toUpperCase() never edit the old string — they hand you a brand-new one.

blocks.title: Greet yourself and count your letters
blocks.instructions: Put YOUR name in the quotes. The program should say hi to you and tell you how many letters are in your name. Then press Check.
blocks.solutionOutput:
Hi, Thanvish!
Your name has 8 letters.
blocks.passMsg: Nice — you joined strings AND measured one. ✍️
blocks.failMsg: Make sure you use + to join the text and name.length() to count the letters.

blocks.title: The secret password (use .equals!)
blocks.instructions: The real password is "java" but the guess is shouted as "JAVA". Use toLowerCase() on the guess and compare with .equals() so the password is accepted. Print "Access granted!" when it matches.
blocks.passMsg: You compared strings the safe way with .equals(). 🔐
blocks.failMsg: Remember: use guess.toLowerCase() to make it lowercase, then password.equals(...) to compare.

challenge.title: Make a text-block award card
challenge.instructions: Build a fancy award card! Use a Java text block (the triple quotes """ ... """) to draw a bordered card, then use String.format with %s and %d to print a line saying who earned how many stars. Make it celebrate someone you like!
challenge.hint: A text block starts and ends with three double quotes """. Everything between them is printed exactly. Then use String.format("%s earned %d stars!", hero, stars).
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 Gorgeous card! You've mastered text blocks AND String.format — Day 3 complete!
